what_it_does: It defines the completion criteria and verification rules for unifying model selection under a single resolver/registry, organized by priority tiers and standing 'one mechanism' criteria, with out-of-scope items explicitly noted.
observations: Every criterion is a checkbox (☐) that must be verified by live use, not by reading code.
Four verification rules are declared: FUNCTION by USE, registry-is-truth, byte-identical, and fail-loud.
Criteria are grouped into four priorities: resolver/registry foundation, cognition-engine routing, interactive/clone routing, and scattered-knob absorption.
Standing criteria M1–M3 demand a single entry point, registry-as-truth, and no silent un-unified selection points.
Out-of-scope concerns are listed explicitly rather than omitted: embedder layer, voice/TTS/STT, and mode-loadout.
Specific model names, endpoints, and file references (e.g., bridge.py:1064, :8000, deepseek-v4-flash, kimi) are used as concrete examples.
Tim is named as the human judge of whether a criterion is 'right'.
standouts: A line is GREEN only when verified BY USE — real runs, spawn trails, or live resolve calls, never code inspection.
U4 is called THE proof Tim asked for: one turn running extractors on the local 4B and synth/judge roles on a cloud brain simultaneously.
U12 states '-pro' is never an implicit default as Tim's rule.
M1 requires every model choice to flow through resolve_model or a documented registry-driven service selector.
conventions: Uses checkbox (☐) markers for each verifiable item.
Mixes normative rules with concrete test cases and grep audits.
References specific code paths and model identifiers as examples.
Explicitly declares out-of-scope items instead of silently dropping them.
concerns: Some criteria depend on external artifacts (run:// trail, live resolve calls) that are not present in this file.
The 'byte-identical verified' rule requires a git diff that may not be reproducible from the file alone.
Specific model names and endpoints may become stale as providers change.
'Tim is the judge of right' introduces a human gate that is not further defined here.
notes: This is the acceptance checklist that turns model-routing unification from an idea into verifiable, use-proven facts.
questions: What is the exact schema returned by resolve_model(intent)?
Where are model_capabilities.json and capability_providers() located?
Who or what is 'Tim' in this context?
What are the precise service identities of the '4B' and the 'cloud brain'?
How is the run:// trail generated and stored?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose — a completion-criteria truth table for model-routing unification — matches the content; it is a priority-ordered checklist with verification rules and explicit scoping.
apparent_themes: Verification by empirical use rather than static analysis
Registry as the single source of truth
Fail-loud behavior over silent wrong-model fallback
One mechanism for all model selection
Explicit scoping and honest out-of-scope notes
intention_signals: It is trying to enforce that all model selection in the build, cognition, and clone paths converges on one resolver/registry, with acceptance proven by live runs rather than code inspection, while leaving sibling concerns deliberately untouched.
novelty: The explicit 'verified BY USE' rule, the 'byte-identical default' constraint for swarm functions, and the named human gate 'Tim is the judge of right'.
contribution: A structured, priority-ordered acceptance checklist with verification rules and explicit out-of-scope notes that a fused system could reuse as a contract for when model-routing unification is complete.
summary_for_embedding: A priority-ordered completion-criteria document that defines how model-routing unification must be verified by live use, with registry-as-truth, single-entry routing, fail-loud behavior, and explicit out-of-scope items.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a truth-table acceptance checklist for unifying all model selection under one resolver/registry and proving it through real runs.
